module: Module 1 |Introduction to Sustainability Matters in SMEs
lecture: 1.2 Sustainability Matters for SMEs: The Foundation
content: Welcome to Module one. Now, while this course is titled Sustainability Measures for SMEs, module one is called Sustainability Matters for SMEs. Here we will try to establish a conceptual foundation. We will begin by defining SMEs in both global and Indian context and tracing their evolution. We will then examine their pivotal role in our economy before analyzing the challenges and opportunities that they encounter. Subsequently, we will situate SMEs within three major policy frameworks, the Viksit Bharat 2047, Mission LiFE, and the Panchamrit Climate Commitments. By the end of this module, you will be able to define and explain SMEs in the global and Indian context, including the historical evolution. You will analyze the significance of SMEs in India's economy and their relationship with sustainability. You will evaluate the sustainability challenges and opportunities that SMEs face. You would be able to understand national policy frameworks such as Viksit Bharat 2047 Mission LiFE and Panchamrit, and the implications for SMEs, and you would be able to apply these sustainability principles to local SME context through very practical observations. Let us start with the basics. SMEs, what are SMEs? They are small and medium enterprises, and they constitute the backbone of the global economy. According to the United Nations, SMEs represent approximately 90% of all businesses worldwide. They generate between 60 to 70% of global employment and contribute nearly half of the global GDP. This illustrates that SMEs are not peripheral actors rather, they are central to economic progress and social development. Consequently, their role in advancing sustainability is indispensable since their collective actions shape global outcomes. In the Indian context, small and medium enterprises are formally recognized as micro, small and medium enterprises. That's why you will hear the word MSMEs being said very often. Their legal foundation rests on the MSME Development Act of 2006, which has undergone revisions to reflect changing economic realities with the most recent thresholds introduced in 2025. I want you to kind of make use of this knowledge in this way. You must go and find out what the initial MSME Development Act of 2006 laid out and why it has undergone these revisions and what have these revisions reflected. I also want you to look at media reporting because you are the book that has been given to you is about how to make sense of media narratives, because the media is the fourth estate in any democracy. It is there to comment upon the government schemes. It is there to reflect upon what society is doing as a whole. So it's very important for you young people to read what the media is writing in the form of the newspaper coverage, television broadcasts, podcasts, or radio interviews, as well as the online space. So I want you to see what the media reported in 2006, about the MSME Development Act and how the newspaper coverage has reflected upon the revisions. For that, you must read opinion pieces, what several important people who are connected to this have written in the form of analytical essays in the newspapers? And try to reflect on how the changing realities of the economy have had implications on these revisions? And what is the most recent revision introduced in 2025? So I want you to reflect on this, and I want you to also comment about this in the discussion forum so that all your classmates and everyone connected to this course knows what is happening and can also do some peer to peer learning in this process. It'll also give you an understanding of why certain SMEs did well in certain periods of time and why certain SMEs failed. One important thing as an entrepreneur is to keep an eye on the trends because demand and supply is something that you have to estimate when you start an SME. So how policy feeds into that? How it is important to look at what the newspaper is criticizing or commenting about certain SMEs is what you need to do, and that's why this book has been given to you as a reference material because there is so much of information out there, but you must know how to bucket those informations into themes. You must know how to make sense and discern these narratives so that it works in your favor. Now under this framework, and I'm referring to the framework here, which was that of the MSME Development Act 2025, the most revised one. Micro enterprises are defined as those with investments of up to 2.5 crore and an annual turnover, not exceeding 10 crore. Small enterprises are characterized by investments up to 25 crore and a turnover of up to a 100 crore. While medium enterprises include those with investments of up to 125 crore and a turnover capped at 500 crore. This classification is not merely statistical, it carries profound implications as it determines which enterprises qualify for targeted policy benefits, institutional credit and government support schemes aimed at fostering inclusive growth and sustainability. India's structured engagement with SMEs began with the MSME Development Act of 2006. However, a transformative milestone was the introduction of Udyam Registration on 1st July, 2020. This initiative simplified the process of formalization by creating a digital, paperless and transparent registration system. Through Udyam enterprises gained streamlined access to credit subsidies and government programs. The reform marked a decisive step in bridging the gap between policy frameworks and the lived realities of small businesses. Do you know that India hosts more than 63 million MSMEs and they operate across manufacturing services and trade? Collectively, they provide employment to over 110 million individuals and contributes substantially to national GDP and exports. It is for this reason that MSMEs are frequently described as the backbone of India's economy, from neighborhood kirana store to the local textile units and technological startups, MSMEs are omnipresent today. When such a vast sector embraces sustainability, the multiplier effect on economic resilience and ecological stewardship is profound. Despite their indispensability MSMEs encounter formidable challenges. The World Bank estimates that India's MSMEs sector faces a credit gap exceeding $380 billion dollars. In addition, many enterprises struggle with outdated technologies, cumbersome compliance requirements and limited awareness of sustainability frameworks. These constraints often restrict their capacity to scale in a way and compete in both domestic and global markets. Yet it is precisely within these constraints that opportunities emerge. While MSMEs continue to face structural and operational constraints, they are also situated within an environment of emerging possibilities. The global economic order is increasingly shaped by sustainability imperatives, the rise of green supply chains, and growing consumer awareness. About ecological issues and ethical issues. International buyers now require enterprises to demonstrate compliance with environmental and social standards, and domestic markets are witnessing a steady increase in demand for products with lower ecological footprints. Within this evolving context, MSMEs that take proactive measures can transcend traditional limitations and establish themselves as agile and future oriented enterprises by embracing. Energy efficient technologies integrating into green supply chains and harnessing the potential of digitization, MSMEs can simultaneously not only strengthen their competitiveness, but they can contribute meaningfully to sustainable development. In an era where consumer awareness about environmental and ethical considerations is steadily expanding and increasing enterprises that embed sustainability into their core operations at a very early stage will be in a better position to leverage. Cultivate the resilience, secure market trust, and achieve enduring competitive advantage. Having examined the scope, the contributions, and the constraints that MSMEs face, we will now shift our focus towards certain national policy frameworks. Policies establish strategic direction. They set ambitious targets and they create incentives for businesses to transform. The three major frameworks that align here for MSMEs with India sustainability agenda is the Viksit Bharat 2047, The Mission LiFE and the Panchamrit Climate Commitments. We will first begin with the Viksit Bharat 2047, which is India's long-term vision for becoming a developed nation. What is Viksit Bharat 2047 en visa? It articulates India's aspiration to achieve developed nation status by the Centenary of Independence, and a structured around three foundational pillars of economic prosperity, social inclusion, and environmental sustainability. I would repeat these again, economic prosperity, social inclusion, and environmental sustainability. Within this framework, MSMEs are envisaged as engines of inclusive growth and innovation. They're expected to diffuse industrialization beyond the metropolitan cities, generate employment and adopt sustainable practices that reinforce national competitiveness. This cannot be coming at a better time when we are seeing our metropolitan centers grow. When we are seeing a rapid expansion of urban infrastructure, we are seeing a rise in our youth population who will need more and more employment opportunities. This eson par framework is something that MSMEs can look at to en visa inclusive growth and innovation. Economic prosperity under the Viksit Bharat 2047 requires MSMEs to operate in an environment of fairness and competitiveness. Tax and trade reforms are designed to ease their operations and integrate them into global value chains. With these reforms, MSMEs can consolidate India's role as a competitive player in global value chains and reduce the dependence on concentrated trade partners. When we talk about social inclusion within the Viksit Bharat framework, social inclusion places, MSMEs at the heart of job creation and skill, they're essential in absorbing India's very young workforce providing not only livelihoods, but also platforms for vocational and entrepreneurial development. MSMEs thus embody inclusive and equitable growth. While these three pillars outline the structural foundations of Viksit Bharat 2047, sustainability must also be understood as a lived practice embedded in daily choices and enterprise behavior. And this is precisely the vision of Mission LiFE, which is what we are moving to next, which means the Lifestyle For Environment Initiative. Mission LiFE seeks to transform sustainability from an abstract policy goal into concrete actions, which can be adopted by individuals, communities, and businesses, including MSMEs. Now a good example where you can look at how Mission LiFE has taken into aspects, of how individuals and communities can contribute to it is to look at the handbook available on the NITI LiFE Mission website, where you will find the most you know, recent examples of individuals and communities who have committed to these goals. And that can, again, give you a host of ideas on how to include this into your you know, very daily choices such as energy consumption or how your enterprise can behave. Mission LiFE, or Lifestyle For Environment, which is LiFE was introduced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi at COP 26. COP is the Conference of Parties and you will find an entire chapter dedicated to COP in the book, prescribed book given to you. I want you to also go through that. So COP 26 in Glasgow on 1st November, 2021. The Prime Minister of India proclaimed this as a mass movement for mindful and deliberate utilization. Instead of mindless and destructive consumption to protect and preserve the environment. Mission LiFE aims to nudge individuals and communities to practice a lifestyle that is synchronous with nature and does no harm to it. Its central premise is that sustainability is not merely the responsibility of governments or corporations, but also of individuals and communities. Mission LiFE aims to transform sustainability into a mass movement by linking personal choices with planetary outcomes. Hence, for MSMEs, whose operations involved daily decisions about energy, water, and waste. This framework is directly applicable. Let's look at energy conservation first. MSMEs often rely on energy intensive processes by adopting LED lighting, for example, or solar panels or energy efficient machinery. They can lower costs and emissions. Energy efficiency constitutes both an ecological imperative and a strategic economic choice. MSMEs that implement energy audits or transition towards renewable sources. Not only mitigate environmental externalities, but also accrue substantial cost advantages. Now let's look at water conservation sectors such as textiles, leather, and food processing units are highly water intensive. Adopting practices like rainwater harvesting, wastewater recycling, and periodic water audits can significantly enhance resource efficiency and promote long-term sustainability. Adopting systematic water management practices enables MSMEs to safeguard critical resources, ensure compliance with evolving environmental standards, and enhance resilience in the face of the intensifying water scarcity. You know, this is a very interesting infographic that you could see on the screen. We are constituting 18% of the world's population, the India, India as a subcontinent itself. Yet we have access to just 4% of fresh water resources. So we are definitely going to face water scarcity, and we have to think of water conservation very deeply. The next aspect is waste reduction. Although MSMEs generate substantial quantities of waste, many of them lack systematic mechanisms for its management. By instituting segregation at source, adopting, recycling, and embracing circular economy principles, waste can be reconceptualized as a valuable resource stream rather than as a liability. Integrating waste minimization and circular economy approaches enhances operational efficiency, reduces cost, liabilities, and strategically aligns MSMEs with contemporary sustainability paradigms. Let's look at sustainable food systems. Agro based MSMEs that prioritize responsible sourcing, optimize cold chain infrastructure, and minimize post-harvest losses contribute not only to food security, but also to the global discourse on sustainable production and consumption. Sustainable food practices help MSMEs boost efficiency and attract consumers who are increasingly becoming aware of eco-friendly choices. Let's look at healthy lifestyle and workforce wellbeing next. MSMEs can encourage in their respective units, safe and sustainable practices, such as cycling to work, safe workplace conditions, and limiting their exposure to hazardous materials, promoting healthy lifestyle choices, trends employee wellbeing, while reinforcing their corporate social responsibility commitments. Many SMEs can have food canteens that have boards that display how much of food is wasted every day and place that in a critical place where people come and leave their empty you know, plates so that they realize how much food they're wasting. As a, unit as a whole, and they can show over a period of time how the food waste is decreasing because of this communication campaign. These are some measures. Now let's look at e-waste management, for MSMEs, engaged in electronics or repair responsible E-waste Recycling and partnerships with certified handlers are indispensable to regulatory compliance and also signal and ethos of environmental stewardship. Adopting E-waste Solutions enhances compliance with regulations and demonstrates environmental responsibility to stakeholders. Sustainability practices, and green supply chains is something that we should also be thinking about. Integrating MSMEs into green supply chains allows them to meet global sustainability compliance, gives them access to global markets, expands the opportunity for exports, and also earns the trust of an increasingly eco-conscious consumer. Thus, Mission LiFE can be seen as a practical guide, yet strategically important framework. It helps MSMEs strike a balance between cost efficiency, compliance with regulations, and continuing to stay relevant and competitive in the marketplace. Additionally, it anchors MSME's roles within India's larger development and sustainability commitments. Let's look at some government schemes, government initiatives provide practical mechanisms for supporting MSMEs. The Udyam Registration, which I referred to earlier, was introduced in 2020, and it has enhanced transparency and simplified access to institutional credit and subsidies. The ZED Certification Scheme launched in 2022, advances the ethos of zero defect, zero effect. It encourages enterprises to improve product quality while simultaneously reducing environmental externalities. Together these schemes operationalize sustainability at the level of enterprise practice itself. Let's look at a case and try to understand this through this case called the Net Zero to Net Hero. India's bold declarations at COP 26 reshaped global perceptions from being regarded as a cautious actor. India emerged as a proactive leader in climate governance. This transition from pledging Net Zero to being recognized as a climate hero offers valuable lessons for SMEs. By embracing sustainability, SMEs two can reposition themselves from reactive participants to being proactive leaders within their prospective sectors. So module one, sustainability matters for MSMEs. You were able to see why sustainability matters. And through this, I would also like you to reflect upon the entire module, make your notes, try to discuss about this on the discussion forum with your peer group. But I also want you to do this activity to contextualize what we learned in module one. I want you to visit either your local Kirana store or a local MSME enterprise or a tailoring unit or a bakery, and I want you to observe their practices in context to how are they using energy, electricity, water, but also waste? What kind of packaging materials are they using? How are they disposing waste? Where are they sourcing their supplies from? We will then reflect on their practices and align our observations with the themes of Mission LiFE. Through this exercise, you will be able to see how sustainability frameworks are completely applicable to everyday business practices. So I hope through this activity you will be able to reflect on what we did. To conclude MSME's are not peripheral, but pivotal to India's economic and sustainable future. In this model, we examined their definitions, contributions, and challenges, and connected them with national frameworks such as the Viksit Bharat 2047, Mission LiFE and the Panchamrit Commitments. We also analyzed garment schemes such as Udyam and ZED that operationalize support. Our central insight is that MSMEs, which innovate and align with sustainability frameworks will be best situated to thrive in a future where competitiveness and continuing to remain relevant and sustainable will become inseparable.
